enrichment.short_description: Open-source, web-based interface for controlling CNC milling machines running Grbl, Smoothieware, or TinyG/g2core.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: CNCjs is a full-featured, browser-based interface for CNC milling controllers. Instead of relying on a dedicated desktop application, it runs as a server (typically on a Raspberry Pi or any machine with Node.js) and streams G-code to the machine's controller board over USB, Bluetooth, or Wi-Fi.

It supports the most common hobbyist and prosumer CNC firmwares — Grbl, Smoothieware, and TinyG/g2core — and includes a 3D G-code visualizer, a widget-based workspace, keyboard shortcuts, and a watch-directory feature for automatically loading new files.

The project is released under the MIT License and is maintained as community open-source software, with desktop wrapper builds available for Linux, macOS, and Windows in addition to the core web server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: CNCjs is aimed at CNC hobbyists, makerspaces, and small workshops who want to run a networked, remotely-accessible control panel for their machine without buying commercial CNC control software.

features.title: 3D G-code visualizer
features.description: Renders the toolpath in 3D so operators can preview a job before cutting.

features.title: Multi-firmware support
features.description: Works with Grbl, Smoothieware, and TinyG/g2core controller boards.

features.title: Remote, multi-client access
features.description: Multiple browser sessions can connect to the same serial port simultaneously over the network.

features.title: Z-probe support
features.description: Built-in probing workflow for setting tool height on the workpiece.

features.title: Watch directory
features.description: Automatically picks up new G-code files dropped into a monitored folder.

features.title: Responsive layout
features.description: Usable on tablets and phones under 720px wide, not just desktop browsers.

pros: Completely free and open source under MIT
Runs on cheap hardware like a Raspberry Pi
Supports several popular hobbyist CNC firmwares
Accessible remotely from any browser on the network
cons: Requires some technical setup (Node.js, serial connection configuration)
No official mobile app, only a responsive web UI
Development is community-driven with no commercial support line

faqs.question: Is CNCjs free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. CNCjs is open-source software released under the MIT License, free to download, run, and modify.

faqs.question: What CNC controllers does CNCjs support?
faqs.answer: It supports Grbl, Smoothieware, and TinyG/g2core controller firmware.

faqs.question: Do I need special hardware to run CNCjs?
faqs.answer: No — it runs on a Raspberry Pi or any computer with Node.js installed, connected to the CNC controller via USB, Bluetooth, or Wi-Fi.

faqs.question: Can multiple people control the machine at once?
faqs.answer: CNCjs allows multiple concurrent HTTP connections to the same serial port, so several browser clients can view or interact with the same session.

use_cases.title: Hobbyist CNC control
use_cases.description: Run and monitor a desktop CNC mill from a browser instead of dedicated desktop software.

use_cases.title: Makerspace shared machines
use_cases.description: Give multiple members remote, browser-based access to a shop's CNC router.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: CNCjs is a free, open-source, web-based control interface for Grbl, Smoothieware, and TinyG CNC machines, self-hosted on a Raspberry Pi or PC.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source web dashboard for controlling and monitoring 3D printers running the Klipper firmware.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Fluidd is a lightweight, responsive web interface built for 3D printers running Klipper firmware and controlled through Moonraker. It gives operators a dashboard for real-time monitoring and control: live temperature charts, a G-code viewer with object-exclusion support, multi-webcam viewing (MJPEG, HLS, WebRTC), and print history with statistics.

The interface is customizable with different layouts, color themes, and a dark mode, and includes a built-in code editor for tweaking config and macro files directly from the browser. A single Fluidd installation can also manage more than one printer.
Fluidd is released under the GPL-3.0 license and built by volunteer contributors, with the project accepting sponsorships to keep development going and the tool free for the Klipper community.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: It's built for hobbyists and small shops running Klipper-based 3D printers who want a fast, self-hosted control panel as an alternative to Mainsail or the stock Klipper web front ends.

features.title: Live thermal monitoring
features.description: Real-time charts for hotend, bed, and chamber temperatures.

features.title: Multi-webcam support
features.description: Displays feeds via MJPEG, HLS, or WebRTC streams.

features.title: G-code viewer
features.description: Visualizes prints with multi-tool and object-exclusion support.

features.title: Built-in file manager and editor
features.description: Upload, search, and edit G-code and config files with syntax highlighting from the browser.

features.title: Multi-printer management
features.description: Control and monitor several printers from a single Fluidd instance.

features.title: Print history and statistics
features.description: Tracks completed prints and surfaces usage statistics over time.

pros: Free and open source under GPL-3.0
Responsive design works well on phones and tablets
Customizable themes and dashboard layout
Active community and regular updates
cons: Requires a Klipper + Moonraker setup, not usable with stock printer firmware
No official commercial support
Some configuration knowledge of Klipper is needed to get the most out of it

faqs.question: Does Fluidd work with any 3D printer?
faqs.answer: No — it's built specifically for printers running Klipper firmware with Moonraker as the API backend.

faqs.question: Is Fluidd free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Fluidd is free, open-source software released under the GPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: Can I run multiple printers from one Fluidd install?
faqs.answer: Yes, Fluidd supports managing multiple printers from a single installation.

faqs.question: How is Fluidd funded?
faqs.answer: It's built by volunteer contributors and funded through community sponsorships rather than sales.

use_cases.title: Remote print monitoring
use_cases.description: Watch temperatures, webcam feed, and print progress from any browser on the network.

use_cases.title: Multi-printer farm dashboard
use_cases.description: Manage several Klipper printers from a single Fluidd instance.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Fluidd is a free, open-source web interface for monitoring and controlling 3D printers running Klipper firmware, self-hosted via Moonraker.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source software that turns a Linux PC into a controller for mills, lathes, plasma cutters, and other CNC machines.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: LinuxCNC is software for controlling CNC machines — milling machines, lathes, 3D printers, laser cutters, plasma cutters, robot arms, hexapods, and more. It runs on Linux, typically with real-time kernel extensions for the precise timing CNC motion control requires, and reads standard G-code to drive machine axes.

It supports several graphical front ends, advanced motion features like rigid tapping and cutter compensation, and works with a wide range of motion-control hardware interfaces (parallel port, Mesa FPGA boards, and others).
The full source code is released under the GNU GPLv2 license. LinuxCNC can be installed on Debian or Ubuntu, or run directly from an official Live/Install DVD or USB image.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: LinuxCNC is aimed at machine builders, retrofitters, and CNC hobbyists who need a full-featured, no-cost motion control system for a wide variety of machine types, from small hobby mills to large industrial retrofits.

features.title: Broad machine support
features.description: Drives mills, lathes, 3D printers, laser cutters, plasma cutters, and robot arms.

features.title: Real-time motion control
features.description: Uses Linux real-time extensions for precise timing-critical machine control.

features.title: G-code interpreter
features.description: Reads standard G-code programs to drive tool paths.

features.title: Advanced machining features
features.description: Supports rigid tapping, cutter compensation, and other advanced CNC capabilities.

features.title: Multiple GUIs
features.description: Offers several graphical front ends to suit different workflows and machine types.

features.title: Hardware interface flexibility
features.description: Works with parallel port setups as well as Mesa FPGA motion control cards.

pros: Completely free under GPLv2
Supports a very wide range of machine types beyond just mills
Long-established project with active forums and documentation
Runs on low-cost Linux hardware
cons: Requires Linux and often a real-time kernel, adding setup complexity
Steeper learning curve than consumer CNC control software
No corporate backing or paid support tier

faqs.question: Is LinuxCNC free?
faqs.answer: Yes, LinuxCNC is free and open source, released under the GNU GPLv2 license.

faqs.question: What operating system does LinuxCNC require?
faqs.answer: It runs under Linux, generally with a real-time kernel extension for precise motion timing.

faqs.question: What kinds of machines can LinuxCNC control?
faqs.answer: Mills, lathes, 3D printers, laser cutters, plasma cutters, robot arms, hexapods, and other CNC-style machines.

faqs.question: How do I install LinuxCNC?
faqs.answer: It can be installed on Debian or Ubuntu, or run from an official Live/Install DVD or USB image.

use_cases.title: Hobby CNC mill control
use_cases.description: Drive a home or garage CNC mill or router using free, GPL-licensed motion control software.

use_cases.title: Industrial machine retrofits
use_cases.description: Replace an aging proprietary controller on an older industrial machine tool.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: LinuxCNC is free, open-source GPLv2 software that controls mills, lathes, plasma cutters, and other CNC machines from a Linux PC.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source web interface for managing and monitoring 3D printers running Klipper firmware.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mainsail is a web-based control interface for 3D printers running Klipper firmware, communicating with the printer through the Moonraker API. It provides real-time temperature and progress tracking, a bed mesh visualizer, a G-code file manager and editor, macro organization with parameter support, and print history with performance analytics.

The dashboard also supports webcam integration, timelapse, control of power devices such as relays and smart plugs, slicer integration, and multiple languages. Mainsail can be installed as part of MainsailOS (a Raspberry Pi / Orange Pi image), via Docker, through manual installation, or accessed through the hosted my.mainsail.xyz option, and it can manage more than one printer from a single dashboard.
The project is released under the GPL-3.0 license and is maintained by community contributors, funded in part through Patreon and GitHub Sponsors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Mainsail suits Klipper-based 3D printer owners — from single-machine hobbyists to small print farms — who want a fast, customizable control dashboard without paying for commercial print-farm software.

features.title: Multi-printer dashboard
features.description: Manage and monitor several Klipper printers from one interface.

features.title: Bed mesh visualization
features.description: Renders bed leveling mesh data graphically.

features.title: Macro management
features.description: Organizes G-code macros with support for parameters.

features.title: Power device control
features.description: Turns relays and smart plugs on/off directly from the dashboard.

features.title: Webcam and timelapse support
features.description: Displays live camera feeds and can assemble print timelapses.

features.title: Print history and analytics
features.description: Tracks past prints along with performance statistics.

pros: Free and open source under GPL-3.0
Multiple installation paths including a ready-made OS image
Rich feature set for print farms (multi-printer, power control, timelapse)
Actively maintained with community funding
cons: Only works with Klipper firmware via Moonraker, not stock printer firmware
No official paid support channel
Initial setup requires some familiarity with Klipper/Moonraker configuration

faqs.question: Is Mainsail free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mainsail is free, open-source software licensed under GPL-3.0.

faqs.question: Does Mainsail work without Klipper?
faqs.answer: No, Mainsail is built specifically for Klipper firmware and requires the Moonraker API to communicate with the printer.

faqs.question: How do I install Mainsail?
faqs.answer: Via the MainsailOS image for Raspberry Pi/Orange Pi, Docker, manual installation, or the hosted my.mainsail.xyz option.

faqs.question: Can Mainsail manage more than one printer?
faqs.answer: Yes, a single Mainsail dashboard can manage multiple printers.

use_cases.title: Klipper printer control
use_cases.description: Monitor and operate a Klipper-based 3D printer remotely from a browser.

use_cases.title: Small print farm management
use_cases.description: Run and track several Klipper printers from a single dashboard with power control and timelapse.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mainsail is a free, open-source web dashboard for controlling and monitoring 3D printers that run Klipper firmware via Moonraker.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source digital asset manager for organizing, tagging, and sharing 3D print file collections.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Manyfold is a self-hosted digital asset manager built specifically for 3D print files. It lets users browse an interactive 3D preview of models, organize a growing library with tags, creators, collections, and metadata like source or print-compatibility notes, and automatically tidy files by renaming and reorganizing them according to that metadata.

The tool can flag duplicate files, nested archives, and inefficient formats to help keep a large model library clean, and it supports sharing collections publicly or privately, including federation with the Fediverse via ActivityPub.

Manyfold is built with Ruby on Rails, PostgreSQL, and Three.js/Mittsu for 3D rendering, and is released under the AGPL-3.0 license. It can be self-hosted via Docker, or tried on a shared public instance run by the project.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Manyfold is for 3D printing enthusiasts and makerspaces with large, sprawling collections of STL/OBJ files downloaded from sites like Thingiverse, MyMiniFactory, or Patreon, who want one organized, searchable library instead of a messy folder tree.

features.title: Interactive 3D preview
features.description: View models directly in the browser before printing.

features.title: Rich metadata and tagging
features.description: Organize models by tag, creator, collection, source, and compatibility notes.

features.title: Automatic file tidying
features.description: Reorganizes and renames files on disk based on customizable metadata rules.

features.title: Duplicate and problem detection
features.description: Flags duplicate files, nested models, and inefficient formats in a library.

features.title: Public/private sharing
features.description: Share collections selectively, including federation over ActivityPub.

features.title: Self-hosted Docker deployment
features.description: Runs as a self-hosted Rails application via Docker.

pros: Free and open source under AGPL-3.0
Purpose-built for the mess of 3D-print file libraries specifically
Automatic tidying saves manual file organization work
Optional public instance available for trying it without self-hosting
cons: Requires Rails/PostgreSQL infrastructure to self-host
Community-run project without commercial support
Best suited to users with sizeable model libraries, overkill for a handful of files

faqs.question: Is Manyfold free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Manyfold is free, open-source software licensed under AGPL-3.0.

faqs.question: Can I try Manyfold without self-hosting it?
faqs.answer: Yes, the project runs a public shared instance that anyone can join in addition to the self-hosting option.

faqs.question: What file formats does Manyfold support?
faqs.answer: It's built around common 3D print formats such as STL and OBJ, with previews and metadata organization for print files.

faqs.question: How does Manyfold organize files?
faqs.answer: Through tags, creators, collections, and metadata, with an automatic 'tidy' feature that renames and reorganizes files based on that metadata.

use_cases.title: 3D model library organization
use_cases.description: Bring order to a large folder of downloaded STL/OBJ files with tags and metadata.

use_cases.title: Collection sharing
use_cases.description: Share curated model collections publicly or privately, including via the Fediverse.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Manyfold is a free, open-source, self-hosted asset manager for organizing, tagging, and sharing 3D print file collections.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source web interface for remotely controlling and monitoring consumer 3D printers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OctoPrint is a web interface that lets users control and monitor 3D printers remotely from a browser. It covers live webcam monitoring, print progress and G-code visualization, temperature monitoring and adjustment, manual head movement and custom commands, job control (start/stop/pause), timelapse recording, and event hooks for scripting system actions.

A plugin system with an official plugin repository extends OctoPrint with community-built add-ons for things like bed-leveling visualization and printer-specific integrations, and user access controls allow multi-user setups with permissions.

OctoPrint is Free and Open Source Software released under the GNU Affero General Public License (AGPL), created and maintained by Gina Häußge, who works full-time on the project and is supported through community and corporate sponsorship.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: OctoPrint is the standard remote-control layer for hobbyist 3D printer owners running consumer printers (originally targeted at RepRap-style and similar machines) who want to monitor and manage prints without standing next to the printer.

features.title: Live webcam monitoring
features.description: Watch prints in progress via a connected camera feed.

features.title: G-code visualization
features.description: Previews the toolpath and tracks print progress.

features.title: Plugin ecosystem
features.description: Official plugin repository for extending functionality with community add-ons.

features.title: Timelapse recording
features.description: Automatically records timelapse videos of prints.

features.title: Built-in STL slicing
features.description: Can slice STL files directly within the interface.

features.title: User access control
features.description: Restricts printer control to authorized users.

pros: Free and open source under AGPL
Enormous plugin ecosystem covering most 3D printer add-ons
Long track record and large community
Works with most consumer 3D printers, not tied to one firmware
cons: Typically requires a dedicated Raspberry Pi or similar always-on host
UI can feel dated compared to newer Klipper-based interfaces like Mainsail/Fluidd
Plugin quality varies since most are community-maintained

faqs.question: Is OctoPrint free?
faqs.answer: Yes, OctoPrint is free and open source, released under the AGPL license.

faqs.question: Does OctoPrint work with any 3D printer?
faqs.answer: It's compatible with most consumer 3D printers, not limited to a single firmware.

faqs.question: Who maintains OctoPrint?
faqs.answer: It was created and is maintained by Gina Häußge, who works on it full-time with community and sponsorship support.

faqs.question: Can I extend OctoPrint's functionality?
faqs.answer: Yes, through its plugin system and the official OctoPrint plugin repository.

use_cases.title: Remote 3D print monitoring
use_cases.description: Watch progress via webcam and control a 3D printer from anywhere on the network.

use_cases.title: Print farm job control
use_cases.description: Manage multiple printers with plugins for queueing, notifications, and timelapses.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: OctoPrint is free, open-source AGPL software for remotely controlling and monitoring consumer 3D printers with webcam and plugin support.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted travel journal and trip planner with an interactive map, itineraries, and travel statistics.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: AdventureLog is a self-hosted travel companion for logging adventures, planning trips, and tracking where you've been. It offers an interactive world map for visited and planned destinations, detailed adventure entries with photos, descriptions, dates and ratings, and trip planning with itineraries, flight information, and packing checklists.

A statistics dashboard shows countries visited, regions explored, and cities logged, and a countries/regions explorer visualizes coverage on a map. Collaborative features let users share trips with friends and family.

Built with SvelteKit and TailwindCSS on the frontend and Django with PostGIS on the backend, AdventureLog is released under the GPL-3.0 license. It can be deployed via Docker, on Proxmox, or on Synology NAS, and the project accepts sponsorships to support development.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: AdventureLog fits travelers who want to keep a private, self-hosted record of trips and travel statistics — without handing their travel history to a third-party SaaS service.

features.title: Interactive world map
features.description: Visualizes visited and planned destinations geographically.

features.title: Adventure logging
features.description: Records trips with photos, descriptions, dates, and personal ratings.

features.title: Trip planning and itineraries
features.description: Plans upcoming trips with flight details and packing checklists.

features.title: Travel statistics dashboard
features.description: Tracks countries, regions, and cities visited over time.

features.title: Collaborative sharing
features.description: Shares trips with friends and family for joint planning or viewing.

features.title: Geospatial backend
features.description: Uses Django with PostGIS for accurate location and mapping data.

pros: Free and open source under GPL-3.0
Full self-hosting keeps travel data private
Combines journaling, planning, and statistics in one tool
Multiple deployment options including Docker, Proxmox, and Synology NAS
cons: No dedicated native mobile app, only a responsive web UI
Requires self-hosting infrastructure and setup effort
Community-maintained without a commercial support tier

faqs.question: Is AdventureLog free?
faqs.answer: Yes, AdventureLog is free, open-source software released under the GPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: Do I need to self-host AdventureLog?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's designed to be self-hosted, with install options including Docker, Proxmox, and Synology NAS.

faqs.question: Does AdventureLog have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: There is no dedicated mobile app; the web interface is responsive for use on mobile browsers.

faqs.question: Can I share trips with other people?
faqs.answer: Yes, AdventureLog includes collaborative features for sharing trips with friends and family.

use_cases.description: Log adventures with photos and ratings on a self-hosted, private instance.

use_cases.title: Group trip planning
use_cases.description: Plan itineraries and share packing lists and flight details with travel companions.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: AdventureLog is a free, open-source, self-hosted travel journal with an interactive map, trip planning, and travel statistics.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source personal flight tracking app that logs and visualizes your flight history on a private server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: AirTrail is a modern, self-hosted personal flight tracking system for aviation enthusiasts who want to log and analyze their flight history without sending the data to a third-party service. It plots flights and routes on an interactive world map and can import flight history from platforms like MyFlightRadar24, Flighty, and OpenFlights.

The app provides searchable flight history, statistics such as total distance, flight time, and airline breakdowns, multi-user support with authentication and OAuth login, and both light and dark themes.
AirTrail is open source, deployable via Docker or Docker Compose on any Linux host, and is developed by Johan Ohly. The project emphasizes that flight data stays entirely on the user's own server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: AirTrail is built for frequent flyers and aviation enthusiasts who want a private alternative to cloud flight-tracking apps, with full ownership of their travel history.

features.title: Interactive flight map
features.description: Visualizes flown routes and destinations on a world map.

features.title: Data import
features.description: Imports flight history from MyFlightRadar24, Flighty, OpenFlights, and other sources.

features.title: Flight statistics
features.description: Calculates total distance, flight time, and airline breakdowns.

features.title: Multi-user support
features.description: Supports multiple accounts with authentication and OAuth login.

features.title: Searchable flight history
features.description: Filters and searches through logged flights.

features.title: Self-hosted privacy
features.description: Keeps all flight data on the user's own server with no third-party tracking.

pros: Free and open source
Full data privacy through self-hosting
Imports data from popular flight-tracking apps
Simple Docker/Docker Compose deployment
cons: Requires self-hosting on a Linux server with Docker
No official managed cloud hosting
Smaller community than more established self-hosted travel tools

faqs.question: Is AirTrail free?
faqs.answer: Yes, AirTrail is free, open-source software.

faqs.question: How do I install AirTrail?
faqs.answer: It's deployed via Docker or Docker Compose on a Linux host.

faqs.question: Can I import my existing flight history?
faqs.answer: Yes, AirTrail supports importing data from services such as MyFlightRadar24, Flighty, and OpenFlights.

faqs.question: Does AirTrail support multiple users?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes multi-user support with authentication and OAuth login options.

use_cases.title: Personal flight logging
use_cases.description: Track and visualize your own flight history privately on a self-hosted server.

use_cases.title: Family flight tracking
use_cases.description: Log and compare flight statistics across multiple user accounts on one instance.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: AirTrail is a free, open-source, self-hosted personal flight tracker that logs and visualizes flight history on your own server.

enrichment.short_description: Privacy-focused, self-hosted location history tracker offering an open-source alternative to Google Timeline.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Dawarich is a location tracking and history visualization app built as a privacy-focused alternative to Google Timeline. Users import location data from sources like Google Takeout, OwnTracks, and GPSLogger, and integrate with photo tools such as Immich and PhotoPrism, then explore it through interactive maps, a daily timeline replay, and a 'scratch map' showing countries visited.

The app includes trip management with photo integration, travel journaling, distance and city analytics, and export to GPX or GeoJSON, along with native iOS and Android apps for tracking on the go and offline sync when reconnected.

Dawarich is open source under the AGPL-3.0 license and can be fully self-hosted for free with all Pro-tier features included. The developer, Berlin-based ZeitFlow UG, also offers an optional managed cloud service with paid tiers: Lite at €49.99/year (12-month history), Pro at €119.99/year (unlimited history, advanced visualizations, API access), and Family at €299.99/year (5 users), each with a free trial.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Dawarich suits privacy-conscious users who want the convenience of a location-history timeline like Google's, but stored on infrastructure they control, as well as users who'd rather pay for a managed cloud instance than run their own server.

features.title: Interactive route mapping
features.description: Visualizes location history and daily movement on an interactive map.

features.title: Timeline replay
features.description: Replays a day's movements chronologically.

features.title: Trip journaling
features.description: Attaches photos and notes to trips for a travel journal.

features.title: Scratch map
features.description: Shows countries visited as a filled-in world map.

features.title: Data export
features.description: Exports location history to GPX or GeoJSON formats.

features.title: Native mobile apps
features.description: iOS and Android apps support offline tracking with automatic sync.

features.title: Third-party integrations
features.description: Works with OwnTracks, GPSLogger, Immich, PhotoPrism, and Google Takeout imports.

pros: Self-hosted version is free with all Pro features included
Open source under AGPL-3.0
Native mobile apps for continuous location logging
Optional managed cloud plan removes the need to self-host
cons: Self-hosting requires running a Rails application and keeping it updated
Cloud plans are a recurring subscription if you skip self-hosting
Full accuracy depends on quality of imported/tracked location data

faqs.question: Is Dawarich free?
faqs.answer: The self-hosted version is completely free and includes all Pro-tier features. A paid managed cloud service is also available starting at €49.99/year.

faqs.question: What license is Dawarich released under?
faqs.answer: It's open source under the GNU AGPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: Can I import my Google Timeline data?
faqs.answer: Yes, Dawarich supports importing data from Google Takeout, along with OwnTracks and GPSLogger.

faqs.question: Does Dawarich have mobile apps?
faqs.answer: Yes, native iOS and Android apps are available, including offline tracking with automatic sync.

use_cases.title: Personal location history
use_cases.description: Replace Google Timeline with a self-hosted, privacy-respecting location log.

use_cases.title: Travel journaling
use_cases.description: Combine location history with photos and notes to document trips.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dawarich is a self-hosted, open-source location history tracker and Google Timeline alternative, free to self-host with an optional paid cloud plan.

enrichment.short_description: Routing and route optimization API and open-source engine for building navigation, delivery, and mapping applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: GraphHopper is a directions and route-optimization platform built on OpenStreetMap data. Developers use its Directions API to add routing, turn-by-turn navigation, geocoding, map matching, isochrones, and multi-vehicle route optimization to their own applications, with pre-built profiles for cars, bikes, scooters, delivery trucks, cargo bikes, and outdoor activities like hiking and mountain biking, plus support for custom vehicle profiles.

The underlying GraphHopper Routing Engine, the jsprit optimization library, and GraphHopper Maps are open source, so teams that prefer to self-host can run the routing engine themselves instead of using the hosted API.

The company, headquartered in Munich, Germany, offers a free non-commercial tier (500 credits/day, up to 5 locations per request) alongside paid Basic (€69/month), Standard (€199/month), and Premium (€479/month) plans that raise daily credit limits, max locations per request, and support level, plus a custom enterprise tier for higher volume.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: GraphHopper serves developers and companies building logistics, delivery, field-service, or mapping applications that need routing and route optimization — from hobbyist projects on the free tier to enterprise fleets on the Premium or custom plans — as well as teams that want to self-host the open-source routing engine directly.

features.title: Routing API
features.description: Calculates turn-by-turn directions across car, bike, scooter, truck, and pedestrian profiles.

features.title: Route optimization
features.description: Solves multi-vehicle, multi-stop routing problems for delivery and field service.

features.title: Geocoding
features.description: Converts addresses to coordinates and back.

features.title: Map matching
features.description: Snaps noisy GPS traces onto the actual road network.

features.title: Isochrones
features.description: Generates reachability areas within a given travel time.

features.title: Custom vehicle profiles
features.description: Lets developers define routing profiles for specific vehicle dimensions and access restrictions.

features.title: Open-source routing engine
features.description: The core GraphHopper Routing Engine and jsprit optimization library can be self-hosted.

pros: Free tier available for non-commercial use
Open-source routing engine can be self-hosted instead of using the paid API
Covers routing, optimization, geocoding, and map matching in one platform
Global coverage powered by OpenStreetMap data
cons: Free tier is non-commercial only and capped at low request limits
Paid plans can get expensive at higher usage tiers
Self-hosting the open-source engine requires infrastructure and OSM data management

faqs.question: Is GraphHopper free?
faqs.answer: There is a free tier limited to 500 credits/day and non-commercial use. Paid plans start at €69/month for commercial use with higher limits.

faqs.question: Is GraphHopper open source?
faqs.answer: The core GraphHopper Routing Engine, the jsprit optimization library, and GraphHopper Maps are open source and can be self-hosted.

faqs.question: What can I build with the GraphHopper API?
faqs.answer: Routing, turn-by-turn navigation, route optimization for multiple vehicles, geocoding, map matching, and isochrones.

faqs.question: Where is GraphHopper based?
faqs.answer: The company is headquartered in Munich, Germany.

use_cases.title: Delivery route optimization
use_cases.description: Plan efficient multi-stop routes for delivery fleets to cut time and fuel costs.

use_cases.title: In-app navigation
use_cases.description: Add turn-by-turn directions and mapping to a consumer or enterprise application.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted routing
use_cases.description: Run the open-source GraphHopper engine on your own infrastructure using OpenStreetMap data.

features: Routing, optimization, geocoding, map matching APIs

enrichment.seo_meta_description: GraphHopper is a routing and route optimization API built on OpenStreetMap, with an open-source engine and paid plans from €69/month.
